caveats: a: The occupation source is broader than this six-digit industry and is dominated by large telecommunications carriers.
a: The estimate excludes tasks already automated, but public sources do not identify their current share in lens firms.
rho: The survey is global, vendor-sponsored, and not limited to independent satellite telecommunications firms.
rho: Reported AI projects do not establish the fraction of exposed labor actually implemented.
e: Public company revenue mixes may not represent privately held firms.
e: The dataset firm count is an estimate and may include establishments whose legal entity is not independently transferable.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions and are not satellite-industry-specific.
s5: Telecom deal counts include large carriers, infrastructure assets, and non-US transactions outside the lens.
q: Recurring revenue share is not a direct measure of retained automation benefit.
q: Contract structures differ between fleet operators, capacity resellers, and managed-service providers.
d5: SIA data are global and cover a broader commercial satellite industry.
d5: Nominal revenue and subscriber counts do not directly measure constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current basket.
o: Direct-to-device and vertically integrated constellation providers could disintermediate some resellers.
o: The estimate does not assume that the same incumbent firm retains the customer, only that a lens-type operator remains required.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Wholesale and enterprise customers buy recurring access, capacity, airtime, managed connectivity, and usage-based transmission services, sometimes alongside equipment or engineering work.
lens.excluded_activities: Direct-to-home satellite television, media networks, satellite tracking stations classified elsewhere, captive network units, shell spectrum vehicles, equipment-only sellers, and businesses outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that provide recurring or repeat satellite signal forwarding, reception, capacity, airtime, or resale services to external telecommunications and broadcasting customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment at three-digit NAICS 517, where installation and repair is 29.67% of employment, office and administrative support is 14.85%, sales is 17.82%, and computer and mathematical work is 16.04%. The estimate maps the documentation, monitoring, support, billing, sales-assistance, and software tasks in those groups to current AI exposure, while treating field repair and accountable network decisions as less exposed. Broad carrier employment probably overstates field labor for satellite resellers and therefore understates exposure for the lens.
caveats: The occupation source is broader than this six-digit industry and is dominated by large telecommunications carriers.
The estimate excludes tasks already automated, but public sources do not identify their current share in lens firms.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from a representative sample of independent US satellite service providers, including current automation status and labor hours by workflow.
a.rationale: AI can substitute portions of NOC alert triage, ticket classification, customer support, billing reconciliation, proposal drafting, forecasting, and routine configuration analysis. Physical ground-station work, radio-frequency engineering, incident command, and safety or regulatory approvals remain primarily human tasks.

d5.bridge: SIA reported 62% global satellite-broadband subscriber growth and 16% broadband-revenue growth in 2025, alongside 4% commercial-satellite revenue growth. Iridium reported 3% subscriber growth and 5% total-revenue growth in 2025. The estimate uses these quantity and revenue indicators to bound US five-year constant-quality demand, sharply tempering recent broadband growth because the lens includes mature wholesale services and because revenue is affected by price and mix. Global growth and a leading operator's performance may overstate US lens demand, while revenue growth can understate quantity growth when unit prices fall.
caveats: SIA data are global and cover a broader commercial satellite industry.
Nominal revenue and subscriber counts do not directly measure constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current basket.
d5.change_evidence: US service-volume series for satellite capacity, active enterprise terminals, billable airtime, and wholesale connections, quality-adjusted and matched to NAICS 517410 revenue.
d5.rationale: LEO capacity, direct-to-device development, resilient connectivity, mobility, IoT, and government demand support greater service quantity. Mature GEO capacity, price compression, customer concentration, and substitution by terrestrial networks limit the base case.

caveats: Public company revenue mixes may not represent privately held firms.
The dataset firm count is an estimate and may include establishments whose legal entity is not independently transferable.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the dataset-injected candidate population with ownership, external-customer revenue, recurring-service mix, normalized EBITDA, and operating-status fields.
e.rationale: The Census definition is centered on services sold to other telecommunications and broadcasting establishments, and satellite operators commonly earn recurring service revenue. Eligibility is reduced for captive units, spectrum or asset shells, equipment-heavy firms, project engineering businesses, and firms whose normalized earnings do not fall inside the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 517410 ($1,194,025,668) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 517410 ($7,027,945,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3470 (margins.json[517]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Telecom. Services EBITDA/Sales 34.70%); revenue/employee $808,739 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 517410); class <5: 256 firms, avg revenue/firm $616,184 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $213,816 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,539,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $881,033 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,371,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,863,824 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,238,194 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,410,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $135,272,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,939,544 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $274,304,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,183,766 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 55

caveats: Direct-to-device and vertically integrated constellation providers could disintermediate some resellers.
The estimate does not assume that the same incumbent firm retains the customer, only that a lens-type operator remains required.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and churn data separating demand lost to self-service terminals, direct fleet-operator sales, bundled terrestrial alternatives, and software-only orchestration.
o.rationale: The service requires an accountable operator to contract for satellite and ground capacity, manage licensed radio links, monitor availability, route traffic, meet service levels, bill customers, and handle incidents. Software can automate these functions but does not eliminate the network operator or reseller relationship for most demand.

q.bridge: Iridium reported that service revenue, described as primarily recurring, was 75% of fourth-quarter 2025 revenue; it also charges service providers and resellers monthly access and usage fees. The estimate treats recurring contracts and embedded connectivity as supporting benefit retention, then discounts for usage-based billing, renewals, customer bargaining, lower-priced plans, and competing capacity. One scaled satellite operator likely has stronger infrastructure differentiation than a typical reseller and therefore may overstate retention.
caveats: Recurring revenue share is not a direct measure of retained automation benefit.
Contract structures differ between fleet operators, capacity resellers, and managed-service providers.
q.change_evidence: Private-company contract samples showing fixed versus usage-based charges, renewal repricing, gross retention, customer concentration, service-level penalties, and observed margin changes after automation.
q.rationale: Fixed access charges and mission-critical service relationships can preserve part of implemented savings, but wholesale customers can demand concessions and falling unit prices or abundant capacity can pass benefits through at renewal.

rho.bridge: NVIDIA surveyed more than 450 global telecommunications professionals: 40% reported deploying AI in network planning and operations, 77% reported operating-cost reductions, and almost all were deploying or assessing AI. The estimate converts reported current activity and perceived impact into the five-year implemented share of exposed work, then discounts for smaller-firm integration, data, reliability, and change constraints. Large-company respondents likely overstate near-term implementation capacity for lower-middle-market firms.
caveats: The survey is global, vendor-sponsored, and not limited to independent satellite telecommunications firms.
Reported AI projects do not establish the fraction of exposed labor actually implemented.
rho.change_evidence: A US satellite-service operator survey documenting production AI workflows, percent of labor hours affected, deployment budgets, exception rates, and realized headcount avoidance.
rho.rationale: Customer-service, billing, sales, and monitoring workflows can adopt mature tools, but fragmented OSS and BSS systems, limited training data, uptime requirements, and human accountability slow full workflow redesign.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Deloitte expected roughly 400 telecom transactions in 2025 and highlighted operator consolidation, while the satellite sector has also seen strategic combinations. The estimate starts near the employer-owner intention rate, discounts uncompleted plans and nonqualifying transfers, and allows an offset for active sector consolidation. The broad owner survey may overstate completed control transfers, while large telecom deal flow may overstate activity among eligible private firms.
caveats: Owner intentions are not completed transactions and are not satellite-industry-specific.
Telecom deal counts include large carriers, infrastructure assets, and non-US transactions outside the lens.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year US transaction database for independent satellite communications service firms, matched to firm size and coded for control transfer, failed process, internal reorganization, and seller ownership age.
s5.rationale: Strategic demand for spectrum, customers, and network capabilities supports transactions, but a small buyer set, national-security review, licensing, customer concentration, and owner dependence can prevent or delay qualifying transfers.

principal_driver: Recurring mission-critical connectivity can support both automation savings and continued need for an accountable operator.
principal_weakness: The eligible firm universe is small and heterogeneous, while the best public operating evidence comes from scaled companies outside the target size band.

title: Satellite Telecommunications
